perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means any tangible or intangible information or materials possessed by a Party in whatever for m or format relating to the Disclosing Party or its actual or proposed information systems, other business, financial or accounting systems, business procedures or methods, business plans, financial products, marketing plans, results of operations, custome rs, markets, prospective customers, contracts (actual and proposed) with third parties or personnel directly or indirectly disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party during or in the course of discussions or correspondence arising out of or r elated to the Transaction whether received before or after the Effective Date. Confidential Information includes all information owned by a third party and disclosed by a Disclosing Party hereunder .
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means information or materials possessed by a Party relating to the Disclosing Party disclosed by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party during discussions related to the Transaction. Confidential Information includes all information owned by a third party and disclosed by a Disclosing Party hereunder .
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'any tangible or intangible information or materials possessed by a Party in whatever form or format relating to the Disclosing Party or its actual or proposed information systems, other business, financial or accounting systems, business procedures or methods, business plans, financial products, marketing plans, results of operations, custome rs, markets, prospective customers, contracts (actual and proposed) with third parties or personnel directly or indirectly', the definition of 'Confidential Information' becomes significantly narrower. This contradicts the later use of the term in Paragraph 7, which implies a broader scope when it discusses the retention and return of 'all copies of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information,' potentially including materials beyond just direct disclosures during discussions. This creates ambiguity as to what information the Receiving Party is obligated to protect and return.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The provisions of this Agreement shall remain in full force and effect beginning as of the Effective Date and shall continue with respect to a Receiving Party for as long as it retains the Confidential Information in its possession and for three (3) years thereafter. A receiving Party shall return or destroy all copies of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to the Disclo sing Party within ten (10) days after the Disclosing Party’s request.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing specific examples of what constitutes such information. The contradicted text requires the Receiving Party to return or destroy all copies of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information. Because the definition of Confidential Information has been narrowed, it is unclear if the receiving party is obligated to return all the information they were initially obligated to return as described in the original text, thus creating a direct contradiction. The omission therefore creates an inconsistency within the document regarding the scope of confidential information and the obligations surrounding it, as defined by the perturbation definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt measures to protect the confidentiality, limited use and proprietary nature of the Confidential Information at least as restrictive as those it adopts for its own confidential information of similar importance and in any event, no less than reasonable; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to only those of its directors, officers, agents, employees, corporate affiliates and third parties retained by the Receiving Party who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof and who are made aware of the confidentiality provisions of this Agreement ( and in the case of a third party, who have signed a binding undertaking of confidentiality and non -use substantially equivalent to this Agreement ), and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized u se or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to its employees who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof, and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'limited use and proprietary nature' and the phrase '( and in the case of a third party, who have signed a binding undertaking of confidentiality and non -use substantially equivalent to this Agreement )' from the agreement, the level of protection expected from the Receiving Party is greatly reduced. The removal of the third party confidentiality agreement directly contradicts Paragraph 4, which anticipates potential disclosure to third parties and implies a mechanism to ensure their confidentiality obligations. The omission raises concerns whether third parties are required to have any binding confidentiality agreements
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party may use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evaluating, negotiating and, in its discr etion, entering into the proposed Transaction. A Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information pursuant to a request by an applicable regulatory authority, or if they are required by law or pursuant to an order of a court of competent jurisdiction to disclose Confidential Information, provided, however, in such event the Receiving Party shall (i) promptly notify the Disclosing Party; (ii) consult with the Disclosing Party as to the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request; and ( iii) if so requested, cooperate with the Disclosing Party in seeking a protective or der or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.justification: The original text ensured third parties who receive confidential information sign a confidentiality agreement. The changed text omits this requirement. Paragraph 4, presented as 'Contradicted Text,' anticipates disclosing confidential information to third parties. By removing the requirement for third-party confidentiality agreements, the changed text directly contradicts the expectation and implied obligation in Paragraph 4, creating a clear in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party may use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evaluating, negotiating and, in its discr etion, entering into the proposed Transaction. A Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information pursuant to a request by an applicable regulatory authority, or if they are required by law or pursuant to an order of a court of competent jurisdiction to disclose Confidential Information, provided, however, in such event the Receiving Party shall (i) promptly notify the Disclosing Party; (ii) consult with the Disclosing Party as to the advisability of taking steps to resist or narrow such request; and ( iii) if so requested, cooperate with the Disclosing Party in seeking a protective or der or other appropriate remedy.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party may use the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evaluating the proposed Transaction. A Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if they are required by law to do so.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'negotiating and, in its discretion, entering into' the proposed transaction, we restrict the Receiving Party's use of the information. Also by removing the regulatory authority request. This contradicts the broader allowance for 'evaluating, negotiating and, in its discretion, entering into the proposed Transaction' originally stipulated. The modified text limits permissible use, creating uncertainty about the Receiving Party's obligations and rights regarding the information beyond just evaluation, because 'negotiating' is removed. This contradicts section 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to its employees who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof, and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the Receiving Party to use Confidential Information for evaluating, negotiating, and potentially entering into a transaction. The changed text removes 'negotiating and, in its discretion, entering into,' limiting the use to only 'evaluating'. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, specifically part (iii) which states the Receiving Party will 'not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4.' Since Paragraph 4 is now limited to 'evaluating' only, it creates a contradiction as the original agreement implied broader use aligning with negotiation and transaction entry, now restricted. Therefore, the modified text introduces a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall promptly advise each other in writing if they learn of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any of their directors, officers, employees, corporate affiliates, agents or by any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall advise each other if they learn of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'in writing' and 'by any of their directors, officers, employees, corporate affiliates, agents or by any third party,' the clause becomes less specific and potentially harder to enforce. This creates an in-text contradiction with Paragraph 3, which outlines specific responsibilities concerning directors, officers, agents, employees, and third parties. The omission makes it unclear whether the notification requirement extends to unauthorized uses or disclosures by these specific individuals and entities, undermining the intended protection provided in Paragraph 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to its employees who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof, and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified notification in writing regarding unauthorized disclosure by specific parties (directors, officers, etc.). The changed text removes 'in writing' and the list of specific parties, making the notification requirement less specific. This creates an in-text contradiction with the contradicted text. The contradicted text explicitly states the Receiving Party's responsibility for unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information 'by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information,' including employees. The changed text, by omitting the reference to specific parties, makes it unclear whether the notification requirement extends to unauthorized uses or disclosures by these specific individuals and entities. Thus the contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of a breach of this Agreement, monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy and in addition to any other legal remedy , the non -breaching Party shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunctive relief. In the event that any Party takes legal action as a result of a breach of this Agreement, the non -breaching Party shall be entitled to recover reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred in connection with such legal action in the event that it prevails in one or more of its claims.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of a breach of this Agreement, monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy. In the event that any Party takes legal action as a result of a breach of this Agreement, each party will cover their own fees and expenses.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and in addition to any other legal remedy , the non -breaching Party shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunctive relief' and 'the non -breaching Party shall be entitled to recover reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred in connection with such legal action in the event that it prevails in one or more of its claims', the remedies available to the non-breaching party are severely limited. The omission of the right to recover legal fees directly contradicts the intention of providing a comprehensive remedy for a breach, which is implied by stating that monetary damages may not be sufficient. This introduces uncertainty about the actual recourse available to the non-breaching party and creates a contradiction within the context of seeking a fair resolution.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties agree that, in the event of a breach of this Agreement, monetary damages may not be a sufficient remedy. In the event that any Party takes legal action as a result of a breach of this Agreement, each party will cover their own fees and expenses.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the non-breaching party's entitlement to equitable remedies, including injunctive relief, and the right to recover reasonable legal fees and expenses. The contradicted text, which is identical to the changed text, now presents a scenario where monetary damages may not be sufficient, but the remedies available are limited. This directly contradicts the initial intent of providing a comprehensive remedy for a breach, thus creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: A receiving Party shall return or destroy all copies of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to the Disclo sing Party within ten (10) days after the Disclosing Party’s request.
perturbation.changed_text: A receiving Party shall return all copies of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information to the Disclo sing Party upon termination of the Transaction.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the option to 'destroy' and changing the timeframe from 'ten (10) days after the Disclosing Party’s request' to 'upon termination of the Transaction,' we create ambiguity and potential contradiction. The removal of the 'destroy' option now contradicts the implications in section 3, where the party must protect the information. By implying protection it should give the option to destroy the information. Now the receiving party has no way to dispose of the Confidential Information, this created a contradiction with the purpose of section 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Receiving Party agrees (i) to adopt reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information; (ii) to disclose Confidential Information to its employees who have a need to know such information in order for the Receiving Party to accomplish the purposes set forth in Paragraph 4 hereof, and (iii) to not use Confidential Information for any purpose except as permitted by Paragraph 4. A Receiving Party shall be responsible to the Disclosing Party for any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information by any party to whom Receiving Party has disclosed such information.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for either returning or destroying confidential information, aligning with the receiving party's responsibility to protect the confidentiality of the information as stated in the contradicted text. The changed text removes the option to 'destroy' and mandates only 'return' upon termination. This creates a contradiction because it may impede the receiving party's ability to 'adopt reasonable measures to protect the confidentiality of the Confidential Information' as they no longer have the option to permanently dispose of it, potentially increasing the risk of unauthorized disclosure. This directly contradicts the purpose of the contradicted section.
